I'm Norense — a storyteller, speaker and builder.

I played professional basketball, survived things that should have broken me, and turned it all into Mind Bully — a platform helping a generation silence their inner critic. I also speak for organizations like the NBA, Amazon, and BET, and partner with brands like Gillette, Toyota, and Paramount.

The story I don't usually lead with.

I'm one of four. A twin. Two older sisters who are also twins. Nigerian parents, raised in Fort Worth, Texas. From the very beginning, my identity wasn't really my own — it was attached to someone else. Same clothes, same shoes, same haircut as my brother. I wasn't Norense. I was half of something. And I spent my whole childhood trying to figure out how to be the whole of something instead.

The first wound

Between the ages of six and eight, I was sexually assaulted by older women in my life. I'm not going to dress that up. It happened, and it made me feel less than. Insignificant. Like I didn't matter, like there was something wrong with me specifically — because I'd look at my twin brother, my sisters, my cousins, and nothing like that was happening to them. Just me.

I didn't have language for it. So I did what most kids in my situation do. I buried it. And I started looking for somewhere — anywhere — to feel like I mattered.

The exit

At eight, my dad — a pharmacist and a preacher — went back to Nigeria for what was supposed to be a temporary job. My uncle was a state governor and had appointed him chairman of the board over the state's hospitals. It was a real position. A big deal. He went, and then his visa was denied on the way back. Ten years. He couldn't come home for ten years.

So now I'm a kid carrying a secret nobody knows about, looking for an identity that isn't my brother's, in a school where everybody's asking "where your daddy at?" — and I have no good answer. So I picked up a basketball. Not because I loved it at first. Because I needed somewhere to matter.

The bet

I went to North Crowley High School in Fort Worth — a guard factory. Willie Warren, Ken Anderson, name after name of guards getting drafted and going to the league. I wasn't a guard. I was a late bloomer, not seven feet tall, and the offers I was getting didn't match what I knew I could be. So in 2013, when my twin brother went off to Texas Tech, I made a decision that scared the hell out of me. I didn't go with him. I bet on myself. I went to prep school instead.

At prep school I was putting up double-doubles against the best teams in the country, and the calls weren't coming. So me and a couple friends snuck into the film room one night and stitched together our own highlight tapes. Just kids trying to get seen. That tape changed my life. Texas Tech offered me, and I went.

The mountain

Five years at Texas Tech. I became captain. We took three different teams to the NCAA Tournament for the first time in 15 years. I broke both of my feet and kept playing. I survived a coaching change that broke half the roster. I lost 40 pounds in a single year. By the time I left, I was the most winning player in Texas Tech basketball history, and we made the program's first ever appearance in the NCAA National Championship game.

The night that broke me

Days before one of the biggest games of my life, my two cousins flew into Lubbock to watch me play. We went out that night. For the first time in a long time, I felt fully significant — my family was there, the city was there, the team was there, and I was the reason. I left them around midnight to go get rest for morning workouts.

Two hours later, an 18-wheeler hit them.

I never saw them again.

I was caught between two voices. Should I keep suppressing this, or should I surrender to it? And I wanted to surrender. Because it should have been three for three — or none of us. Why was I the only one still here?

I wanted to take my own life. I really did. The only thing that kept me from doing it was the team. The legacy of the guys who came before me. The thought of Keenan, Zach, Justin — the brothers I couldn't quit on. So I made a different choice. I chose to finish the mission. To honor them by shining. And then I did the only thing I knew how to do. I suppressed it deeper and kept moving.

Germany

2020. I'm playing professionally in Germany during the pandemic. More money than I'd ever seen in my life. A three-story house. A prestigious club. By every metric a kid from Fort Worth uses to measure success, I had arrived.

And it nearly killed me.

The coach there ran me through drills on a torn hamstring just to see if I would break. He made me play through a concussion. Curfew at 9pm. No family within thousands of miles. And the suppression I'd been running on since I was six years old — the strategy that had carried me through assault, abandonment, broken feet, dead cousins, and a national championship run — finally stopped working.

So for the first time in my life, I did the opposite of fight. I surrendered. Back to my faith. Back to my foundation. Back to the kid I was before I learned I had to perform to be loved.

Mind Bully

That surrender became Mind Bully. I launched it in 2021 with a single conviction: every human being is fighting an inner critic, and that fight can be won. Not by suppressing it. Not by performing harder. By naming it, surrendering the right things to God, and refusing to let a voice that isn't from the Father tell you who you are.

Three plus years in, we have not missed a single weekly episode. Not one. 300K+ followers. 20M+ video views in 90 days. And the messages I get from listeners aren't "I liked the episode." They're "your show saved my life."

Why I'm still here

I don't want to be remembered as a basketball player. I don't want to be remembered as the guy from the Final Four. I want to be remembered as someone who used the gifts God gave him to make people feel like they don't have to shrink to be loved. That they're significant just because they're breathing. That the voice telling them they're not enough is a lie, and there's a louder voice if they'll just get quiet enough to hear it.

I am who God says I am. And God says I'm significant. He knew me before the beginning of time. I'm the only one with my footprint, my handprint, my walk. So why would I fold and try to be like you, when He made you to be you and me to be me?

I should not be here. By the math of my own life, I should have been broken by now — at six, at eight, at eighteen, at twenty-three, at twenty-eight. I'm still here because of grace, and because somewhere along the way I stopped trying to suppress what was true and started trying to surrender it instead.

That's the whole message. That's the whole platform. That's the whole reason any of this exists.

And we're just getting started.

← Back

The Court.

Before the microphone, there was the game. A decade of basketball forged the man running Mind Bully today — and every chapter below taught me something the world couldn't.

Fort Worth, TX

Where it started.

Son of Nigerian immigrants. A twin searching for identity. My father's visa was revoked when I was eight — he wouldn't return until I was eighteen. I did what survivors learn to do. I suppressed it all.

And I picked up a basketball. Because I needed to matter.

2014 — 2019

Texas Tech.

Captain of the program. I broke both feet and kept playing. Survived a full coaching change. Lost 40 pounds in a single year. And became the most winning player in Texas Tech basketball history — leading three different teams to the NCAA Tournament for the first time in 15 years.

April 2019 — Minneapolis

The National Championship.

Texas Tech's first ever NCAA National Championship appearance. Against Virginia. In front of a nation. The biggest stage basketball has to offer.

Days before tip-off, I took my two cousins out for a night in Lubbock. They had flown in from out of state just to watch me play. For the first time in a long time, I felt significant. I left them at midnight to get rest for morning workouts. Two hours later, an 18-wheeler took their lives.

I wanted to take my own life. I didn't. I chose instead to finish the mission — to honor them by shining.

So I suppressed it deeper. And I kept moving.

2019 — 2020 · Germany

Going pro.

More money than I'd ever seen. A three-story house. A prestigious club. The pandemic hit the world and I hit my breaking point at the same time — alone, overseas, and finally out of things to distract me from the weight I'd been carrying since I was a kid.

The suppression stopped working. And for the first time, I didn't fight my way through. I surrendered. Back to my faith. Back to my foundation. Back to the kid I was before I learned I had to perform to be loved.

2021 — Present

From the court to the mic.

The game gave me the discipline. The resilience. The team. The platform. The calluses. Mind Bully is what I built with all of it.

Same fight. Different arena.
